schools.summary: The number twelve as perfection (six doubled) and three-into-four (Trinity to the four quarters), heaped with OT/NT types; named two-and-two as yoke-fellows; one evil among them (Judas) that the Church learn to bear with the wicked.
This is the mission, not the choice, of the Twelve (Lk 6:13); the number suggests the twelve tribes; exousia = authority as well as power; only healing is named here but preaching is added (v.7).

The Twelve foreshadow the Church's restoration as new patriarchs; preaching and miracles given in immediate connection, so miracles are seals of doctrine — the Papists err in separating word from miracle.
Christ makes them confident by giving strength for the ministry, fulfilling the charge to pray the Lord of the harvest; ranks them not by dignity.
Henry: they were kept first in probation, then in preparation; the best preparative is communion with Christ; the power is levelled against Satan's kingdom.
Twelve answering to the patriarchs/tribes; divine gifts do not make human learning useless (Clarke).

outliers.note: Stresses the wide difference between Christ's having power and the apostles' merely imparting it 'with confession of their own weakness' (Acts 3:6).

schools.summary: 'Apostle' = one commissioned/herald; first so called now they are sent; the four lists agree Peter first and Judas last, fall into three fixed groups of four, with Peter/James/John the inner circle; named in pairs as sent out.
Peter was first among the apostles but without authority over them; Calvin: Rome's primacy doctrine is 'extreme folly,' availing nothing till they prove wicked apostles are Peter's successors.
Simon first only as the first called to constant attendance (Wesley); disciples before apostles — taught of God before sent of God (Clarke).

debates.question: Does Peter's being named first establish a primacy?

sides.position: acknowledged preeminence as spokesman, but no supremacy
no authority over the rest; Rome's claim is folly

schools.summary: Identification of the men: Bartholomew (Bar-Talmai) likely Nathanael from his place in the lists; Lebbaeus/Thaddaeus/Judas one man with equivalent names; Alphaeus = Cleophas (Jn 19:25).
Clarke: Matthew, writing of himself, owns 'the publican' and lets Thomas precede him — the disciples preferring one another in honour; Wesley identifies Lebbaeus as Judas the brother of James.
'The Canaanite' = the Zealot (Heb. kana, 'zealous'; Lk 6:15), a party that struck down open offenders after Phinehas; 'Iscariot' most likely Ish-Kerioth, a man of Kerioth in Judah (Josh 15:25).
Clarke: render 'even he who betrayed him,' not 'who also,' lest others seem to have betrayed Christ too.

sides.position: man of Kerioth (geographical)
from a word for strangulation, given after he hanged himself

outliers.note: Hears a prophetic omen in 'Iscariot' via the tribe of Issachar ('a booty'), figuring the betrayer's reward.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: sent only after sufficient proof of his power; the restriction a kindness, training them in Judaea 'as in a palaestra' and sending physicians to those who reviled Christ.

Jerome/Gregory/Hilary: the Jews preached to first that they have no plea of rejection; Israel, having had more warning through the Law, has less excuse; the Samaritans were settled Gentiles at enmity with the Jews.
Calvin: the order rests on covenant — Christ the minister of circumcision (Rom 15:8), the gospel confined to Abraham's seed till after the resurrection it spread to all nations.
Henry: the Gentiles not to have the gospel till the Jews refuse it (Acts 3:26); the restraint bound only the first mission, afterwards 'teach all nations.'

outliers.note: Reads the charge to preach (not handle money) as guarding against Judas, who had the bag, using the power for gain.

schools.summary: 'House of Israel' = the whole nation then in the land (or the elect among them); 'lost sheep' straying without a shepherd (Ezek 34:5; Isa 53:6); Robertson: 'lost' said in pity, not blame.

Calvin: the first rank given the Jews as firstborn and in covenant; yet under this figure Christ teaches the condition of the whole race — if the covenant-heirs are 'lost' till saved in Christ, how much more those without the prerogative.

themes: lost sheep of the house of Israel

schools.summary: The message identical to the Baptist's and Christ's (Mt 3:2; 4:17); itinerant heralds proclaiming good news; Ellicott suspects a fixed formula announced in the town gate, then a call to repentance.
Calvin: the proclamation arouses the nation to expect an approaching, divine redemption — heavenly and eternal, not earthly and fading.
Chrysostom (note partly displaced onto John's disciples) stresses Christ knew the secret thoughts of all and set right their understanding.

themes: kingdom of heaven is at hand

schools.summary: 'Raise the dead' omitted by the best MSS, matching the absence of any such disciple-miracle before the resurrection; 'freely give' is stronger than English — 'give gratis' — yet never forbids competent support (1 Cor 9:14; 1 Tim 5:18).

Calvin: the healings bear an analogy to Christ's office of universal blessing; the apostles are channels of God's bounty, and 'all hirelings basely corrupt' the office; Clarke calls preaching for hire 'infamous sacrilege.'
Remigius allegorizes the sick (slothful), lepers (unclean in sin), daemoniacs (under the devil); the gift received without money is to be given without price.
Chrysostom hears in 'freely received' a check on pride and a guard against filthy lucre.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Gehazi's sold cure (2 Kings 5:26), making the anti-simony point from the negative side.

themes: heal the sick, cast out devils

schools.summary: The tense of 'provide' (ktēsēsthe, 'do not acquire') means they might take money they had but not get more or delay for it; 'in your purses' = in the hollow girdle used as a purse; gold/silver/brass a descending scale.
Calvin: the injunction is restricted to the temporary mission, not a perpetual rule; Clarke: anxiety in Christ's messenger is a double crime, distrusting the Master bound to support him.
Chrysostom: forbidding possessions pulls up the root of evil, freeing the apostles from suspicion and care for their one work of teaching — 'a happy exchange.'

Jerome glances at the begging philosophers and gives a figurative sense (gold=sense, silver=words); Hilary spiritualizes girdle, scrip, coats, shoes, staff; Augustine: the precept grants a privilege one may decline (so Paul worked with his hands).

debates.question: Is the no-provision rule perpetual or for this mission only?

sides.position: restricted to the temporary commission
a standing counsel of evangelical poverty/detachment

schools.summary: Harmonizes Matthew/Luke (no staff) with Mark (a staff, sandals): go as you are, trusting Providence, procuring no second staff/shoes; scrip = traveller's food-wallet (1 Sam 17:40); two coats = a change of raiment.
Chrysostom/Augustine: 'the workman is worthy of his meat' makes support a due — a labourer's hire, a soldier's stipend — that they need not be ashamed to receive necessaries.
Calvin/Clarke: the minister has a divine right to maintenance, but only maintenance, not to make a fortune.

outliers.note: Notes the same saying grounds both Christ's no-provision command and Paul's organised stipend (1 Tim 5:18) — 'the same law fulfils itself in many ways.'

themes: the workman worthy of his meat

schools.summary: Chrysostom: 'worthy' means able to furnish food, not high rank (so Christ lodged with a publican); they must seek out by report, not yet having power to discern, and abide in one house to avoid suspicion of lightness.

Jerome: choose the host by the neighbours' report, that the preacher not be disgraced by an ill-charactered entertainer who 'rather receives than does a favour'; Ambrose: the caution binds the guest, not the host.
Barnes/Clarke: 'there abide' against wandering like vagrants — ministers profit by laborious use of time in study and prayer, not idle chitchat or the tables of the rich.
Calvin grounds the rule in haste: begin with the better-prepared, and do not change lodging lest you burden any, that you may run on to the next city.
'An house' should be 'the house' (the worthy one found); 'house' = family (Jn 4:53); the salutation is 'Peace be to this house' (Lk 10:5); religion keeps the common courtesies (1 Pet 2:17).
Clarke: Hebrew shalom comprehends all blessings, so to wish peace in Christ's name is 'a positive promise' of all the good implied; Wesley: peace = all blessings.
Calvin: the friendly greeting is 'an opening to conversation' and a trial whether the family will hear.

outliers.note: Glosses the salutation as a prayer 'that all resistance to the truth may be pacified.'

schools.summary: The returning peace is a Hebrew idiom from gift-giving (Ps 35:13): received, it benefits; refused, it returns to the giver, the blessing falling back on the persecuted messenger (Mt 5:10); 'if worthy' allows for inquiry that may fail since they came as strangers.

Calvin: ingratitude unfits them for the supplicated blessing, so break off communication; but it is over-refinement to read the verse of the reconciliation between God and men rather than the customary Jewish wish of well-being.

themes: let your peace come upon it
a real gift given or withdrawn

schools.summary: Shaking off the dust draws on the rabbinic view that heathen dust defiles; a significant act declaring the place profane and below the Gentiles, done literally by the apostles (Acts 13:51; 18:6); a rule for this special tour.
Hilary: the shaken dust leaves an eternal curse and renounces all fellowship; Rabanus: the dust is the earthly thought the preacher gathers, turned as a witness against the despisers' damnation.
Calvin: the verse shows the gospel's worth as the scepter of Christ's kingdom — no crime more offensive than contempt of his word; the threat also comforts the apostles not to fail (2 Cor 2:16).

Sodom and Gomorrha, destroyed for wickedness (Gen 19:24), yet less guilty than gospel-rejecters who sinned against more light (Mt 11:24); 'day of judgment' wider than the final, like the OT 'day of the Lord.'
Calvin: refers to the final account, not Jerusalem's fall; to reject the gospel is the accumulation of all crimes, so the despiser's punishment exceeds Sodom's.

sides.position: the final judgment / a wide OT-style day of the Lord
a particular temporal judgment on that city

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the proofs of life eternal by faith and by the preaching of the gospel.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: 'in the midst of wolves' shows his power, that the sheep convert rather than are destroyed; the dove's nature is not to retaliate, anger quenched by meekness.
The preacher must suffer evil, not do it (Gregory); the serpent guards its head as we guard Christ/the faith (Jerome); wisdom and simplicity must be joined lest either deceive or endanger (Remigius).

The serpent = caution in avoiding danger, the dove = innocence; the paradox of being at once 'supremely guileful and absolutely guileless' (akeraioi = unmixed); cf. Paul 'caught men with guile' (2 Cor 12:16).
Calvin: the verse opens the discourse to the whole apostleship; wolves = enraged enemies; the serpent's caution regulated so as to be neither too timid nor too sluggish.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on 'One Lord Jesus Christ' and the many titles of Christ (door, way, sheep, shepherd), citing the rational sheep sent among wolves.

schools.summary: 'Men' = the wolves, esp. the rulers; the plural 'councils' = lesser local tribunals, not the great Sanhedrin; scourging by the synagogue triumvirate, forty save one / thirty-nine (Deut 25:3; 2 Cor 11:24; Acts 22:19), inflicted within the building.
Calvin prefers 'men' indefinite (against Erasmus's 'these men') — a caution to foresee danger, not a terror that stops duty; the contests reach far beyond the first journey.

debates.question: Does 'beware of men' mean the wolves just named or men in general?

sides.position: these particular men (the persecuting rulers)
indefinite — men in general, among whom all is snares

schools.summary: A striking proof of Christ's prescience: 'governors' = Roman magistrates (Gallio, Felix, Festus), fulfilled through Acts; 'against them' is rather 'unto them,' the dative of those addressed — a testimony to Jews and Gentiles, not necessarily hostile.
Origen: this saying, like 'brother shall deliver brother,' applies perhaps not to the Apostles alone but to all who would believe.

The apostles raised to the rank of prophets: 'you utter the words, but it is I who speak' (Remigius; 2 Cor 13:3); we offer only our will, the Spirit ministers the answer (Jerome); as Abraham giving up Isaac lacked no ram (Hilary).
Chrysostom: where dispute is among friends we 'are ready' (1 Pet 3:15), but before the awful judgment Christ ministers aid that they speak boldly.

'Take no thought' = be not anxious (Mt 6:25); a promise of both what and how to speak, for the impossible-to-prepare hour — not a licence to neglect study and prayer (Clarke, Robertson); fulfilled in Peter and John (Acts 4:13).

Calvin: the Spirit suggests words to those who distrust their own strength; witnessed in martyrs 'almost devoid of talent' who spoke miraculously — yet a measure of care remains, to supplicate for the Spirit.

themes: take no thought what to speak

schools.summary: They speak, yet words and matter are dictated by the Spirit — efficient cause to their instrument (Gill); an extraordinary promise literally fulfilled to the first preachers, opening the NT dispensation, though 'in a measure' the Spirit still enables disciples (Clarke); 'your Father' added for confidence.
Ellicott's caution: the words are strong but it is beside the discourse's drift to build on them 'a theory of inspiration' covering the written records.

outliers.note: Stray fragment ranging the one Spirit's titles — Comforter, Spirit of Truth, Spirit of God, Spirit of the Father.

schools.summary: Betrayal by kin pains more than from strangers, adding 'the pain of lost affection' (Gregory); no true affection between those of different faith (Jerome); comfort placed first, then the alarming perils (Gloss).

The prophecy scarcely credible but for history — only opposition to God could make parents and children deliver up their own to racks and flames (Barnes, Clarke); reproduces Micah 7:6, followed there by waiting on God.
Calvin: such division is not peculiar to believers — a father may rightly pursue an apostate son by holy zeal (Deut 13:9); rightly regulated affections would leave no other just cause of hatred.

'All men' = all kinds, or the greater part, not without exception; hated purely for Christ's name (which stands for the person, Acts 5:41); the persecution runs through the ages — always for the Name (Ellicott, Tertullian; 1 Pet 4:16).

Chrysostom: many begin hot and grow cool, so Christ looks at the end and makes perseverance their own work; Remigius/Augustine: 'the end' may mean perfection — to endure 'in Christ' (Rom 10:4), abiding in faith working by love.
Calvin: the promise of salvation is sheer encouragement — those certain of victory should abide to the last; Clarke alone hears a surviving of Jerusalem's destruction.

debates.question: What is 'the end' to which one must endure?

sides.position: the end of life / of the trial — perseverance in the faith
perfection / endurance 'in Christ' (Rom 10:4)
survival to the destruction of Jerusalem

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Christ bids them not court persecution but flee, adapting to a beginning; Augustine guards flight as instructing weakness not to tempt God, yet a pastor must not desert a flock under common danger; Remigius: endurance for the strong, flight permitted the weak.

Flight is a command, not bare permission — driven from one place, try another with unabated zeal (Calvin); martyrdom not to be courted but danger used wisely (the serpent's wisdom; Polycarp, Cyprian).

Lives not to be thrown away where they can be kept without denial; the 'coming' variously read; Clarke even floats 'ye shall not have instructed the cities'; Robertson lists the views, refusing the charge that Jesus erred.

debates.question: What is meant by 'till the Son of man be come'?

sides.position: the resurrection and the power of the Spirit
the destruction of Jerusalem
the second coming in glory (cf. Dan 7:13), the remnant of Israel called after the fulness of the Gentiles
this very Galilean tour — Christ overtaking them

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the sign of Jonah and the three days in the heart of the earth.

themes: till the Son of man come

schools.summary: Chrysostom: consolation against loss of good name, from Christ's own example; not 'slaves' but 'those of his household,' to show how dear they were (Jn 15:15).
Hilary: we embrace as glory being made like our Lord in suffering; Remigius/Gloss: the disciples will not seek worldly honour while they see the Master pursue redemption through contumely.
Henry: Christ is teaching Master, ruling Master, Master of the house with despotic power in the church; his hard usage should engage us to expect and bear the like (Jn 13:16; 15:20).

Jerome: Beelzebub = the idol of Ekron, 'god of flies' (2 Kings 1:3), the foulest of idols applied to the prince of demons; Remigius/Gloss: if the master is so reviled, the household must not expect better nor be indignant.

Henry: the astonishing inversion — Satan's enemies branded his friends, his servants posing as his foes; persecutors must 'dress up in bear-skins' those they bait, but the hidden shall be revealed; wonder at both men's wickedness and Christ's patience.

Henry: 'nothing covered' read two ways — the gospel's certain publishing to all nations (Acts 2:11), and the clearing of the slandered servants' innocency at the great day (1 Cor 4:5), 'a resurrection of names.'
'Fear them not' grounded in the enemy's limited power (they kill but the body), looking to v.28; the disclosure both emboldens the witness and steadies him under reproach (Rom 8:35).

themes: nothing covered but shall be revealed

schools.summary: Henry: whatever the hazard, publish the everlasting gospel — the enemies seek to suppress it; the apostles deliver 'that, all that, and nothing but that' which they received (Heb 2:3); private instruction now proclaimed openly (Acts 1:3).

Darkness/light = Christ's comparatively private teaching of the Twelve vs. the open proclamation now committed; the flat housetop the place of the Gentiles' reception (Acts 10:9); the whole counsel must be revealed (Acts 20:27).

Henry: the persecutors' power is limited to the body; the soul is killed only when separated from God's love, beyond their reach (Rom 8:35); the remedy is to fear God, able to destroy both in hell (2 Thess 1:9) — even the best saints fearing him (Gen 31:42).
The holy fear of God is the cure of the unholy fear of man — better to fall under all the world's frowns than God's (Isa 51:12); obey God rather than men (Acts 4:19).

themes: fear not them which kill the body
fear him who destroys soul and body
the fear of God vs the fear of man

schools.summary: Argues from less to greater: if the sparrow falls not without God, the immortal need not fear (Jerome); Augustine takes the numbered hairs to teach that nothing of the body is lost in the resurrection, though the shorn hair need not return to its place.
The assarion = a tenth of the denarius; the rabbinic proverb that 'a bird is not taken without Heaven, how much less man'; providence extends to the minutest things.

Calvin: each creature is under God's hand, nothing left to chance — the will of God against the philosophers' 'contingence,' yet not Stoic fate; providence to be viewed as a ground of confidence and prayer.

debates.question: How is the sparrow's fall to be understood — chance, or particular providence?

sides.position: a special, particular providence over each creature
a general providence working through fixed laws (the will of God)

outliers.note: Stray fragment marshalling the verse to prove the Father of Christ is the Maker of the world, against heretics who sever Old and New Testaments.

schools.summary: A proverbial expression of God's perfect knowledge and exquisite care; he has fixed the number of the hairs, so much more will he guard the life and soul; a particular providence directing all to salvation (Rom 8:28), not mere general care.
Ellicott guards against pressing the hyperbole: even the most trivial incidents work together for good to those that love God; the disciples never uncared-for.

From the lesser to the greater: if providence reaches sparrows and a single hair, much more the lives of useful ministers; Clarke: none can estimate a soul Christ bought with his blood, against the deistical 'equal eye' on hero and sparrow.
Calvin: men's preeminence over the creatures is general (Ps 8:6), but this peculiarly concerns the sons of God, whose higher rank arises 'solely from the undeserved kindness of God.'
Chrysostom: the Greek 'confess in me' shows confession is by grace from above, not our own strength; the reward manifold more than the deed.
As we witness to him on earth, so he to us in heaven (Hilary); confession of the mouth necessary unto salvation (Remigius, Raban; Rom 10:10), access to the Father being through the Son.

Confession = acknowledging Christ in every circumstance and before all men (Barnes), a 'firm, manly, public profession' (Clarke); the Aramaic idiom 'confess in me' = unity with Christ (Robertson); reaching beyond the apostles to all (Ellicott; Lk 12:8).

Calvin: confession is a main part of divine worship — if earthly princes call subjects to arms, believers should maintain their King's glory; the base refusal weighed against Christ's matchless testimony before God and the angels.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on how the angels behold God only as far as they can bear, the Son and Spirit alone knowing the Father fully.

themes: Christ confesses us before the Father

schools.summary: To deny = to disown discipleship, prefer worldly interest and men's friendship to God; to be renounced by Christ is to have him as Judge and witness against us, not Advocate; the accusative marks a complete, public, final breach (Robertson).

Ellicott/Wesley: an eternal law with its counterpart of woe; the denial meant is one persisted in, as the saving confession is the constant language of faith and love (Calvin: such names blotted from the book of life).

outliers.note: Stray fragment (shared with v.32) on the angels' limited vision of God.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: 'the most perfect peace' comes when the diseased is lopped off — as the physician amputates and Babel's evil peace ended in good discord; the war is of the world's plotting, not Christ's purpose; he says 'sword' to rouse, not hide the difficulty.

Rooted in Micah 7:6 — a holy war breaking an unholy peace (Jerome); the household division read mystically of the new man severed by the sword of the Spirit (Eph 6:17) from his old nature, or the Church against the Synagogue (Hilary, Augustine).

The sword is the effect, not the design — the wickedness of men, not the gospel, the cause (Barnes; Isa 9:6); 'a bold dramatic climax,' no peace at any price (Robertson); connected with the sword through Mary's soul (Lk 2:35, Ellicott).

debates.question: Of what 'sword' and division does Christ speak?

sides.position: the spiritual division the gospel provokes (Micah 7:6)
primarily the literal Roman sword and Jewish civil wars

outliers.note: Stray fragment on John baptizing with the Spirit and fire, citing 'I am come to send fire on the earth.'

schools.summary: Christ explains the sword as 'intestine divisions, domestic broils' from Micah 7:6; the opposition is between the spirits, not the persons (Clarke); dichasai = divide in two, family ties yielding to loyalty to Christ; perhaps a personal application (Zebedee, Peter's household — Ellicott).

Calvin: tumults arise contrary to the gospel's nature through wicked men; what Malachi 4:6 says of the Baptist applies to all Christ's ministers, yet division is not uniform — every lawful bond is confirmed by unity of faith; only, be not alarmed when it comes.

From Micah 7:6; the gospel times painted in the prophet's dismal colours; Clarke: the rabbis' own sayings of Messiah's days (Sotah, Sanhedrin) show Jesus to be the Messiah, fulfilled in the desolations Josephus records; household enmity works both ways and in subtler forms (Quesnel).

The right ordering of love (Song 2:4): love kindred after God, but where they compete with God, 'hatred of our kindred becomes love to God' (Jerome); the ascending scale — Christ preferred to parents, then children (Gloss).
Chrysostom: obey parents only in things not hurtful to piety (Deut 33:9); even one's own life must be hated, leading to the higher doctrine of the cross.
Christ must be loved supremely or not at all (Barnes); Luke 14:26's 'hating' explained — where affections collide the weaker gives way, acting 'as if he hated' those still loved (Ellicott).
Calvin: not laying aside affection but regulating all love to assign the highest rank to piety (Phil 3:8).

outliers.note: Presses the balancing truth — honour earthly parents too, since Christ said not 'he that loveth father or mother' but added 'more than me'; obey them save when contrary to God.

schools.summary: The condemned carried his own cross to execution (Jn 19:17); to take it up = to endure whatever is burdensome, disgraceful, or trying in following Christ — doing duty, not courting trouble (Barnes); first mention of cross in Matthew, perhaps a prevision of his own (Robertson, Ellicott; Jn 3:14).

Gregory: 'cross' from torment, borne by abstinence or by compassion — but only when joined to 'followeth me,' not for ostentation; Hilary: the cross is where we suffer, die, and rise with him (Gal 5:24).
Calvin: the general law — no disciple without readiness to endure afflictions; the consolation that 'in the cross we are the companions of Christ.'

outliers.note: Stray fragment (shared with v.37) on honouring earthly parents.

schools.summary: The paradox turns on two senses of 'life' (= 'soul,' v.28): to save the temporal is to lose the eternal, and to lose it for Christ is to find life everlasting (Barnes, Ellicott); the saying repeated in four forms (Robertson; Jn 12:25); Clarke instances Cranmer.

Remigius: 'life' = this present state; he who so loves its joys as always to keep them prepares his soul for damnation; Raban joins it to the cross — he who seeks immortal life does not hesitate to lose this one.
Calvin: the over-cautious, thinking their life well-defended (Ps 49:18), will be disappointed and die; those who surrender to die for Christ find their life pass into a better.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Christ giving his back to the scourges and his face to spitting (Isa 50:6).

themes: find your life and lose it
lose it for Christ and find it

schools.summary: To receive the apostle is to receive the Father and the Son (Chrysostom); Christ's Mediator's office — he dwells in them, God in him (Hilary); it is the office not the person that matters, the host losing no reward though the guest be unworthy (Jerome).

To receive Christ's servant 'as a prophet' shows approval of his cause (Barnes); a consolation that some would receive them kindly (Gill); the apostles as Christ's representatives, sent as he was sent by the Father (Ellicott; Jn 20:21; Heb 3:1).
Calvin: how dearly Christ loves them, placing to his own account the kind offices done them (Mt 25:40).

themes: he that receiveth you receiveth me

schools.summary: 'In the name of a prophet' = because he is a prophet (a gospel preacher), received from no ulterior motive (Robertson; eis onoma); the distinction of prophet and righteous man implies a higher inspiration in some, others setting the pattern of a righteous life (Ellicott); the reward belongs to the future kingdom.
Gregory: he who supports the prophet, freeing his voice for righteousness, becomes 'a free partaker in his righteousness'; Hilary: the honour is to the title of disciple, not to a sinner.
Calvin: commends all who truly worship God and love the gospel; the reward corresponds to the rank of the person honoured — a proof of Christ's high esteem for all his disciples.

outliers.note: Cites the rabbinic saying that to receive a learned man into one's house is as if one received the Shekinah; the host loses no reward even if the teacher prove worthless.

themes: receive a prophet in the name of a prophet

schools.summary: The 'little ones' are the lowest, poorest disciples, 'learners, not yet teachers'; the point is the motive — to give cold water 'because he was a Christian' shows love to the Saviour himself (Barnes); even the poorest may thus do works of mercy, for the name of Jesus makes mean services of high worth (Clarke).
Jerome: 'cold water' lest poverty plead lack of fuel — removing even that excuse, so the giver loses no reward; the Gloss: God looks more to the pious mind of the giver than to the abundance given.
Ellicott extends the words by Mt 25:40 to every act of kindness done in the name of the humanity Christ shares with those he calls his brethren (Heb 2:11).
